stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] A 74-year-old client with atrial fibrillation reports nausea and seeing 'yellowish' halos. After reviewing the client's electronic health record, the nurse prepares to act. Complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: Situation: At 07:30, I'm calling about the 74-year-old client in room 201 with atrial fibrillation. He is reporting nausea and seeing 'yellowish' halos around lights. Background: He has a history of CHF and atrial fibrillation, managed with Digoxin 0.125mg and Furosemide 40mg daily. He was admitted for dizziness. Assessment: His heart rate is 52/min. The cardiac monitor shows sinus bradycardia with PVCs. His latest labs show a potassium of 2.9 mEq/L and a digoxin level of 1.8 ng/mL. Recommendation: I believe he is experiencing digoxin toxicity exacerbated by hypokalemia. I recommend holding the next dose of digoxin and administering potassium chloride as ordered.

tabs.title: ECG & Electrolyte Trend
tabs.content:
Assessment ItemCurrent FindingPrevious (2 days ago)
Serum Potassium (K+)2.9 mEq/L (Low)3.8 mEq/L (Normal)
Serum Digoxin1.8 ng/mL1.2 ng/mL
Cardiac RhythmSinus Bradycardia w/ PVCsAtrial Fibrillation w/ RVR

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 07:30
Blood Pressure: 102/64 mmHg
Heart Rate: 52/min
Respiratory Rate: 20/min
SpO2: 96% on room air

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: Cardiovascular: Bradycardia, irregular rhythm, distant heart sounds, peripheral edema +1. Neurological: Client reports blurred vision and seeing yellow halos around lights; oriented to person, place, and time; muscle weakness noted in lower extremities. Gastrointestinal: Reports nausea and anorexia; abdomen soft, non-tender; normoactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-Ray: Mild cardiomegaly is noted. Lungs are clear without consolidation or effusion. No acute bony abnormalities are identified.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Nursing Priorities: 1. Monitor cardiac status and prevent arrhythmias. 2. Restore potassium levels to normal range. 3. Prevent falls related to muscle weakness and dizziness. Nursing Interventions: 1. Continuous cardiac monitoring; assess for signs of digoxin toxicity. 2. Administer potassium chloride IV as prescribed; monitor serum potassium levels closely. 3. Implement fall precautions; assist with ambulation; ensure adequate lighting. 4. Monitor intake and output; assess for fluid overload.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Current Medications: - Digoxin 0.125 mg PO daily (Hold per order) - Furosemide 40 mg PO daily - Potassium Chloride 20 mEq IV PRN for K+ < 3.5 mEq/L (Administer now)

actionOptions.text: Hold the scheduled dose of Digoxin
Administer Potassium Chloride (KCl) IV as prescribed
Administer Furosemide to increase renal clearance
Increase the dose of Digoxin to control the heart rate

hoverRationales.c1: This is incorrect. The client's digoxin level is 1.8 ng/mL, which is within the therapeutic range. The toxicity is potentiated by low potassium, not a pure overdose from an excessive serum level.
hoverRationales.c2: This is correct. Hypokalemia increases myocardial sensitivity to digoxin by allowing more digoxin to bind to the Na+/K+ ATPase pump. This leads to toxic effects like bradycardia and visual disturbances, even with a therapeutic serum level.
hoverRationales.c3: This is incorrect. The client's potassium level is low (2.9 mEq/L), indicating hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia. Hyperkalemia is a risk with potassium replacement, but it is not the current condition.
hoverRationales.a1: This is a correct action. Withholding digoxin is a priority to prevent further binding to the Na+/K+ ATPase pump, which would worsen myocardial irritability and bradycardia. This allows time for the underlying hypokalemia to be corrected.
hoverRationales.a2: This is a correct action. Correcting hypokalemia is crucial as potassium competes with digoxin for binding sites on the Na+/K+ ATPase pump. Restoring normal potassium levels reduces digoxin's effect, mitigating cardiac toxicity and arrhythmias.
hoverRationales.a3: This is an incorrect action. Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes potassium loss. Administering it would worsen the client's hypokalemia and exacerbate the digoxin toxicity.
hoverRationales.a4: This is an incorrect action. Increasing the digoxin dose would worsen the toxicity. The client is already bradycardic (HR 52/min) and symptomatic, indicating the current dose is too high for their metabolic state.
hoverRationales.p1: This is a correct parameter. Continuous cardiac monitoring is essential to detect and manage potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, such as severe bradycardia or ventricular dysrhythmias, which are common complications of digoxin toxicity.
hoverRationales.p2: This is a correct parameter. Monitoring serum potassium is critical to ensure the level returns to the therapeutic range and to prevent overcorrection leading to hyperkalemia. The effectiveness of the potassium infusion depends on these results.
hoverRationales.p3: This is an incorrect parameter. While magnesium and calcium imbalances can also affect cardiac function, the primary and most urgent electrolyte imbalance to monitor in this specific scenario is potassium.
hoverRationales.p4: This is an incorrect parameter. While intake and output are important for any client receiving IV fluids, the most critical parameters to monitor for immediate safety are the cardiac rhythm and potassium levels.

pearls.description: Hypokalemia increases the risk of digoxin toxicity, even when digoxin levels are within the therapeutic range.
pearls.strategy: Always assess potassium levels in patients taking digoxin, especially if they present with signs of toxicity.

pearls.description: Visual disturbances, such as seeing yellow halos, are a classic sign of digoxin toxicity.
pearls.strategy: Instruct patients to report any visual changes immediately.

traps.description: Assuming digoxin toxicity is solely due to elevated digoxin levels without considering electrolyte imbalances.
traps.strategy: Always evaluate electrolyte levels, especially potassium, in conjunction with digoxin levels when assessing for toxicity.

traps.description: Administering potassium too rapidly can lead to hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest.
traps.strategy: Administer potassium chloride slowly and monitor potassium levels frequently.

mnemonics.description: D - Disturbances in vision (yellow halos), I - Irregular heart rate, G - GI upset (nausea, vomiting)
Hi (High) Digoxin sensitivity with Po (Low) K (Potassium)
